title: Breitmann in Turkey
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Derr Breitmann hear im Turkenreich
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vas fighten high und low,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Steh auf, oh Schwackenhammer mein!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Itן¿½s dime for us to go.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Zieh dein Kanonenstiefel an,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und schleife Dir das Schwert,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Schon lang her han mer nichts gethan,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Der Weg ist reitenswerth.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Oopon vitch side? I hartly know
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Boot von side in dis war:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dere ist de holy Russ-land
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All mit a holy Tsar;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But I pe not a holy-er,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor you von Saint, I fear;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Out line is holy ploonder,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mit sacred Lager-bier.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Dereן¿½s von Constantinoble-man
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vot write to me, und say
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He kits me an commission
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To make me Breitmann Bey,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und if I mounts de turpan
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und keeps de Muslin law,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und bribes ein wenig, den I rise
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To Breitemann Pasha.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Dis much is drue, dat Toorkey is
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A real Powder land,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und if deyן¿½re goinן¿½ to touch it off,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vy, ve moost pe on hand.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und if ve shpring into de airs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vhile meddlinן¿½ in de fuss,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I rader dink some Russian bears
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vill shpring along mit us.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und ven he kit to Turkreich
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Der Breitmann work like mad,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und kit ein corps togeder,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mein Gott! vat men he had!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mit Polers und mit Shipsies,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ungaren, Turks, und such,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und allerlei Gesindel. ן¿½Hei!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Says Hans: ן¿½dis beats de Dutch!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Den onwards to his Schicksal
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und forvarts troo de night,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und oopwarts to his mission,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und downvarts in de vight.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Until in de Bulgן¿½ren
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Von night his horse he strode,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und meet a tausand Kossacks
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pefore him on de road.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Slap forward rode der Breitmann
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Right on de Kossack spears,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But forvarts coom deir leader
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And halted his careers,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und gry, ן¿½O Turkisch Ritter,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I am de Capit?n,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And if you want a shindy,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Step up, and Iן¿½m your man.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dey fightet like der teufel,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dey fightet mit deir swords,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und Breitmann vould hafe kilt him,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But ן¿½twas not on de cards,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For de Kossack fire a bistol
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As his retreadt pegan,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Down from his horse all senseless
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Flop! went der Breitemann.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vhen he hafe kit his senses,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Der Breitmann find he lay
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Insite a nople castell,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Upon a canapן¿½;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und py his side a lady
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So wunderschן¿½n to see,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vas shlisin oop a lemon
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Indo a cop of thן¿½e.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Den to himself say Breitmann,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Aldough he hold his jaw,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Dis is de vinest womans,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Py Gott! I efer saw.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vot lofeliness! vot muscle!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mit efery himmlisch charm!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She measures twenty inches,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bei Donner! roundt de arm.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De lady see his glances
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So noble und so game,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und yust as he reflected
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She dink of him de same,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und she say, ן¿½Wie gehts?ן¿½ in English,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Du galiant cavalier,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who art pecome de captive
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All of my bow und spear.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I am a gal dis morninן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yestreen I vas a knight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old hoss you nearly smashedme,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I guess, in that small fight;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And if I hadnן¿½t shot you
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I think I should have ran.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Gottshimmel mit Potzbomben!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Egsclaim der Breitemann.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But say, O nople lady,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vot got you in dot set
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of plackgards vilt dou dell me?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De dame rebly: ן¿½You bet!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My father came from Boston,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when this war began
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He got a splendid contract,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All with the Russi-ן¿½n,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To sell the army shoe-strings;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But I have read of fights,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I dream of war and glory,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For I go for womenן¿½s rights;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then I read a book of poems
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which fairly turned my head,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The ballads of Hans Breitmannן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ohֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ho!ן¿½ Hans Breitmann said.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And as I think the Breitmann
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Must be the greatest man
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who ever went a-fighting
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Since History began,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I dressed me like a soldier,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For I am stark of limb;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With Breitmann for a model,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And try to act like him.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Oh, tell me, noble captive,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While rolling in this storm
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which men call life, hast ever
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Beheld Hans Breitmannן¿½s form?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, could I once embrace him,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And gaze into his eye,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And feel his arms around me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then I would gladly die.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He is the man of mortals,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Odin of them all,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A higher Incarnation,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The ן¿½Menschheitsidן¿½al,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A being made to worship,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To me an earthly Gottן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Py shings!ן¿½ exglaim Hans Breitmann,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Dis ding is gettin hot!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½O laity! nople gountess!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dis man of whom you dink
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ish lyinן¿½ here pefore you,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Half tead for want of trink,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Likewise for lofe of you, too,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Done up mit lofe and durst,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und mit de two togeder,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I donן¿½t know vitch is vorst.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And dou canst safe dy hero
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From bitter Todespein,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If dou hast in de Keller
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Only one Fass of wein.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nay, doubt not in my pocket
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is dot vitch brofes de man,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My bassport, und drei tavern bills
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Against der Breitemann.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De laity she emprace him
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oontil he nearly bust.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Potz-blitz!ן¿½ gasp out der Breitmann,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½She is a squeezer yust!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De damן¿½ she vas vealty,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Likewise an orphan too,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mit a castel und a titel,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So Breitmann put it troo.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So soon the paar vere marrit,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hei! vot a dimes dey had!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hei! how dey life togeder
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So clorious und clad!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now he has cot a titel
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dot was a Capitן¿½n;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hier hat de tale ein Ende
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Herr Count Breitemann.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Charles G. Leland, titled "Breitmann in Turkey", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
